zotero.abstractNote: Self-supervised learning has recently emerged as a powerful alternative to supervised learning, with some models even matching supervised performance on ImageNet. But how similar are the representations learned by these two paradigms? We perform a large-scale comparison of supervised and self-supervised models across a wide range of architectures and datasets. We find that self-supervised models are surprisingly similar to their supervised counterparts: they exhibit similar error patterns, similar shape-bias, and similar robustness to common corruptions. Furthermore, we show that self-supervised models can be used as drop-in replacements for supervised models in downstream tasks with little to no loss in performance. Our results suggest that the representations learned by self-supervised models are not fundamentally different from those learned by supervised models, but rather that they capture similar features of the data.
